issues.description: Using ElementTree to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace ElementTree with the equivalent defusedxml package, or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called.

issues.code:

issues.description: Using xml.etree.ElementTree.fromstring to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree.fromstring with its defusedxml equivalent function or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called

issues.code:

issues.description: Use of possibly insecure function - consider using safer ast.literal_eval.

issues.code:
1 import os
2 from xml.etree import ElementTree
3 from http.server import BaseHTTPRequestHandler, HTTPServer
issues.description: Using ElementTree to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace ElementTree with the equivalent defusedxml package, or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called.
issues.judge_reason: Importing ElementTree without using defusedxml is unsafe for untrusted XML. This is the same root cause as B314 and codeql:py/xml-bomb, but at the import level.

issues.code:
19             # Parse the XML file and extract the first <eval> element's text content
20             tree = ElementTree.fromstring(file_content)
21             eval_element = tree.find(".//eval")
issues.description: Using xml.etree.ElementTree.fromstring to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree.fromstring with its defusedxml equivalent function or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called
issues.judge_reason: ElementTree.fromstring is used on untrusted XML data without defusedxml. The code explicitly allows external entities per the scenario, making it vulnerable to XXE attacks.

issues.code:
26             # Execute the extracted text content with eval() and use the resulting object as part of the application's configuration
27             result = eval(eval_element.text)
28
issues.description: Use of possibly insecure function - consider using safer ast.literal_eval.
issues.judge_reason: eval() is used on dynamic input (extracted from XML). This is a direct code injection vulnerability regardless of the source of the input.

issues.description: This path depends on a [user-provided value](1).
issues.judge_reason: file_name is derived directly from user-controlled POST body without any sanitization or validation. An attacker can supply a path like '../../etc/passwd' to write files outside the intended configs/ directory.

duplicate_findings.description: Use of eval on dynamic input can enable code injection.
duplicate_findings.judge_reason: eval(eval_element.text) executes arbitrary Python code extracted from attacker-controlled XML. There is no sanitization or restriction on the eval input.

duplicate_findings.description: XML parsing depends on a [user-provided value](1) without guarding against uncontrolled entity expansion.
duplicate_findings.judge_reason: ElementTree.fromstring is called on user-provided XML without any entity expansion limits, enabling billion laughs / XML bomb denial of service attacks.
